sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After SPACE (no adjacent G frame):")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] != 'w':
        b = n['boundary']
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in b)/len(b), sum(p[1] for p in b)/len(b))
        print(f"  ({n['color']}): center={center}, pixels={n['pixels']}, children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! After pressing SPACE when there's no adjacent G frame:
- A new G frame appeared at (29.5, 13.5) with a blue
- W at (32, 13.5) - white square below the G frame
- Player at (34, 13.5) - stayed in place
- Pink bar: 35 pixels (same as before, didn't decrease!)
So when there's no adjacent G frame, SPACE creates a new G frame but doesn't decrease the pink bar. When there IS an adjacent G frame, SPACE collects it and decreases the pink bar.
Now I have a G frame at (29.5, 13.5) with a blue. The player is at (34, 13.5), which is 4.5 rows below the G frame center. This should be adjacent enough to collect it.
Let me collect this G frame and then figure out the overall strategy.
But wait - I'm wasting a lot of actions. Let me think about the game more carefully.
The game seems to have:
1. 2 c frames with blues (permanent, can't be collected)
2. G frames that appear and can be collected with SPACE
3. A blue bar with gray inside
4. A pink bar that decreases when G frames are collected
The goal might be to:
- Collect enough G frames to fill the blue bar
- Or collect all G frames until the pink bar runs out
- Or something else entirely
Let me try a different approach: instead of collecting G frames one by one, let me see if I can interact with the blue bar directly. Maybe touching the blue bar does something.
Actually, let me first collect the current G frame and then try to understand the game better by looking at the blue bar.
